# Questions in Livestream 175

Live Stream #175: Meta the Meta, Meta Responses

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:22</a> | Was Solomon's Egyptian father-in-law more likely Siamun or Shoshenq II? | He leans toward Siamun. | He places Solomon's marriage alliance against the reigns overlapping Solomon, especially Siamun, Shoshenq II, and Shoshenq I. Because 1 Kings connects the marriage to Solomon's building projects and his wife staying in the city of David until the palace was complete, he dates the event near the early building period and says Siamun is his best guess, while allowing Shoshenq II as a possible alternative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=956" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:56</a> | Does the church's historical testimony have a lesser, relative authority under Scripture? | Yes. He keeps Scripture as final authority while still granting church councils and tradition real subordinate weight. | He sides more with Gavin Ortlund's framing than with a bare "nuda scriptura" approach. Scripture remains the final authority where "the buck stops," but church councils, apostolic tradition, the church fathers, and learned commentaries still function as real subordinate authorities that help interpreters understand the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=1246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:46</a> | Did the prophecy in Jeremiah 43:10-13 happen? | He discusses the prophecy but says he cannot provide the requested source during the stream. | He cannot supply the requested source during the stream, but he argues the prophecy likely happened through Babylonian military action rather than Nebuchadnezzar personally entering Egypt. He points to Babylonian control of Pelusium and describes the prophecy as fulfilled in an army striking Egypt, taking captives, plundering temples, and leaving, while noting that details such as Heliopolis remain hard to reconstruct archaeologically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=1695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:15</a> | What do you think of translating the Exodus chariot term as "third man charioteers" based on Akkadian war texts? | He rejects the argument, noting surviving Egyptian chariots are two-person chariots and treating the proposed reading as dubious. | He says the proposal appears to turn the term for "officers" into "third man," but the Egyptian evidence does not support three-person chariots. Surviving chariots from Thutmose III's period and Tutankhamun's tomb are two-person vehicles with a driver and archer; officers were charioteers in their own chariots, not extra riders in the same chariot. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=2387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:47</a> | What criteria should be used when ranking doctrines in theological triage? | He prioritizes doctrines tied to historic Christian essentials, especially resurrection, Trinity, salvation, the fall, and Christ's life. | He identifies first-tier doctrines as those that define historic Christian identity, especially the bodily resurrection, virgin conception and birth, the Trinity, salvation by grace, the fall, and the second coming. He frames them around two major issues, the status of humanity and the identity of God, and treats denial of these essentials as moving outside the Christian faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=2887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:07</a> | What do you think of Gavin Ortlund's criteria for theological triage? | He thinks the criteria are generally good, while noting the historical-church-testimony criterion can become more complicated. | He approves the questions about biblical clarity and importance to the gospel, and he sees historical church testimony as useful for doctrines where the church eventually comes to unity. He warns that asking about a doctrine's effect on the church today can be twisted by the spirit of the age, so historic revelation and the gospel should not be overridden by current cultural pressure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=3303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:03</a> | Why does Elihu go unmentioned in God's rebuke in Job 42? | He does not treat that omission as proving Elihu was wholly right; the silence does not settle the issue by itself. | He argues Elihu is indirectly rebuked because the Lord answers out of the whirlwind immediately after Elihu's speech and asks who darkens counsel by words without knowledge. He rejects speculation that Elihu was right, a later addition, or a christophany, and instead reads Elihu as a young, naive participant whose correction is handled differently from Job's older friends. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=3657" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:57</a> | Is the Bulletin for Biblical Research supplement series good scholarship? | Yes. He calls the Institute for Biblical Research a solid, more conservative scholarly group and notes he has published in the series. | He answers yes and explains that the series is connected with the Institute for Biblical Research, which he describes as a solid scholarly organization tending more conservative than SBL. He notes that IBR is selective and that he has a piece in one of its supplements, so his judgment comes from direct familiarity with the series. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=3780" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:00</a> | What do you think of Heiser's Old Testament hermeneutic that starts with ancient Near Eastern context? | He approves starting with the text's original context before moving to later christological readings. | He says the right approach is to start with the original historical and cultural context and then move toward Christological fulfillment. He criticizes purely christocentric readings as often lazy because they bypass the Old Testament symbols on their own terms, but he also warns that a Christotelic approach should not ignore later revelation; his preferred formula is to start Christotelic and end christocentric. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=4183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:43</a> | What is the best commentary on Exodus? | He says the one he reaches for first is Nahum Sarna's JPS Torah commentary on Exodus. | He does not claim Sarna's JPS Torah Commentary is necessarily the best, but says it is the first one he reaches for when checking Exodus. He likes it as a good starting point, while noting that Sarna sometimes alludes to ancient Near Eastern or Egyptian parallels without spelling them out, which can be frustrating. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=4400" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:20</a> | Which commentary gives plenty of information on Exodus 6:3? | He says he would need to check across commentaries and does not have a specific recommendation ready. | He declines to name one from memory, saying he would have to look across his commentaries to see which treats Exodus 6:3 best. The transcript supports only that limited answer, not a positive recommendation for a specific volume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=4460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:20</a> | Would you rather have an Exodus commentary that ignores Egyptian connections or one written by Scott Stripling? | He chooses the former, saying Stripling would not produce a good commentary on the topic. | He chooses the commentary that ignores Egyptian connections because he thinks Stripling would be too doctrinaire about the Exodus to produce a good commentary. In the same discussion he also criticizes some other Exodus commentary work as weak or speculative, including remarks about Eugene Merrill, the NET Bible notes, and William Propp. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=4565" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:05</a> | Is there a commentary about commentaries? | Yes. He mentions older guidebooks to commentaries by D. A. Carson for the New Testament and Tremper Longman III for the Old Testament. | He says such guides did exist, though they are now somewhat out of date. He identifies D. A. Carson's New Testament guide and Tremper Longman III's Old Testament guide as books that evaluated other commentaries, essentially functioning as "meta-commentaries." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=4673" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:53</a> | Is the Transfiguration connected to Exodus 24 and 1 Kings 19? | He declines the question as too broad and asks for a narrower proposed connection. | He says he looked at the passages but could not tell what specific connection the questioner had in mind. Because the prompt joined two passages without explaining the proposed link, he declines to answer and asks the questioner to narrow it to one passage and identify the claimed connection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=5092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:52</a> | Has *Ark of the Covenant* been used as course literature? | Yes. He says Carmen Imes used it in a Biola course. | He answers yes and says his book was used in a course at Biola by Carmen Imes. The remark comes after he discusses Egyptian ritual furniture and carrying-pole parallels to the Ark of the Covenant, which he says are treated in that book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=5535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:15</a> | Did you ever interact with Gordon Fee? | No. He says they were never in the same place at the same time, similar to his missed opportunities to meet Heiser. | He says he never interacted with Gordon Fee and, as far as he knows, was never in the same room with him. He compares this to Michael Heiser, where he was often at the same place a week before or after Heiser but never overlapped with him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=5664" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:24</a> | Why does God not heal amputees? Is that an argument against God? | He says God can heal but does not always do so, and suffering can still be caught up into God's purposes. | He says God can heal amputees, but does not always heal every condition, and that this is not an argument against God. He frames the issue in terms of God's sovereignty and glory, using examples of suffering or disability being used in ministry to say that God may work through weakness rather than remove it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=5859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:39</a> | Do Mark 10:11-12 and Matthew 19:8-9 contradict each other about divorce? | He reads them in context of Jesus' exchange with the Pharisees and does not treat them as a simple contradiction. | He reads both passages in the setting of Pharisees testing Jesus about whether a man may divorce his wife for any cause. Mark emphasizes the creation one-flesh principle and the wrongness of divorce and remarriage, while Matthew includes the immorality exception; he treats the texts as part of the same argument rather than an easy contradiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=7076" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:56</a> | Should pastors raise up elders or deacons first? | He says leaders should raise people organically and watch whether they prove fit for elder or deacon work. | He advises raising people organically rather than forcing one office first. Deacons may emerge as people meet practical needs, while elders require clearer evidence of qualification; in both cases churches should move carefully because unqualified leaders can damage the church in different ways. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=7228" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:28</a> | Why do people use Joshua in Zechariah to argue that Messiah will be a high priest? | He cannot locate the exact passage from the prompt and says the question needed a more specific reference. | After some confusion over Zechariah 2 versus Zechariah 3, he says Joshua the high priest is not Joshua son of Nun and warns against conflating people just because they share a name. He reads Joshua in Zechariah as typological, a type that points forward to Messiah, not as a christophany or the Messiah himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=8838" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:18</a> | Could you explain what functional creation means? | He says he is not sure what the question means and cannot answer it clearly. | The transcript support for this row is limited in the inspected span: he does not have a clear enough definition of "functional creation" from the prompt to answer it. The safe expansion is therefore that he cannot explain the concept without a more precise question or definition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=9734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:14</a> | Do you need recommendations when applying for a funded PhD program? | Yes. He says PhD programs generally require recommendations, usually from former professors. | He answers that recommendations are required not just for funded programs but generally for PhD applications. He says applicants usually need recommendations from former professors, and commonly about three of them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=9795" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:15</a> | Is Jude affirming the prophecy from Enoch in Jude 14-15 as historical? | He says Jude is clearly using 1 Enoch, but that does not settle every question about the text's canonical or historical status. | He says Jude is clearly drawing from the Book of Enoch, which he classifies as pseudepigrapha rather than Scripture. His point is that an apocryphal or pseudepigraphal work can still preserve or express a true point, and Jude as inspired Scripture can use that truth without making all of Enoch canonical or historically reliable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=10053" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:33</a> | Is the church still wealthy? | Not necessarily. He says some churches or individuals have wealth, but many churches also face people trying to exploit benevolence funds. | He says not necessarily: some individuals or churches may have wealth, but churches also deal with people trying to extract money from benevolence funds. He illustrates this with examples from church work and says churches need policies, verification, and a members-first approach so aid is not allocated simply to whoever tells the most persuasive story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=10595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:35</a> | What should Christian men look for in a future wife during dating? | He stresses being equally yoked in convictions, goals, and practical life direction. | He says dating should test whether the two people are genuinely on the same page: faith, biblical convictions, raising children, where to live, work and travel expectations, character, strengths, weaknesses, and availability. He treats dating as a serious information-gathering process and urges resisting premature emotional commitment until those matters are known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=11018" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:38</a> | Is marrying a robot wife acceptable for a Christian marriage? | No. He says a robot is not human and frames the idea as idolatrous. | He rejects the idea because a robot is not a human being and is therefore ineligible for marriage. When a viewer suggests it falls under idolatry, he agrees that this is probably the right category for the moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=11331" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:51</a> | Was monism a thing in the ancient world? | Not really. He says pantheism is probably the closest ancient analogue. | He answers that monism was not really an ancient-world category in the way the question suggests. The closest parallel he offers is pantheism, but he does not develop a broader historical account in this answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=11607" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:27</a> | Is the Virgin Mary the Mother of God and the Queen of Heaven? | He affirms Theotokos language for the incarnation while carefully distinguishing that from saying Mary is mother of the Trinity. | He separates the two titles. He affirms Mary as Theotokos, mother of the God-man and of the second person of the Trinity through the incarnation, while denying that she is mother of the Trinity; he rejects "Queen of Heaven" as an identification of Mary, saying Revelation 12's woman is drawn from Joseph's dream symbolism and refers to Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=12205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:25</a> | Did Egyptians have wedding anniversaries? | He says we do not know, and describes Egyptian marriage as mainly a civil arrangement. | He says there is no evidence that Egyptians celebrated wedding anniversaries or birthdays. Marriage in Egypt appears to have been a civil, contractual arrangement rather than a religious ceremony, and at least early on could be made or dissolved in a comparatively casual verbal way unless children or property complicated matters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=13112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:32</a> | Have you read any of Randy Alcorn's books, fiction or nonfiction? | No. He says he has not read Alcorn's books and cannot comment on the angelic-character material. | He says he has not read Randy Alcorn's fiction or nonfiction and therefore cannot evaluate the question about angelic characters referring to God as Elyon. He adds only that he may look into current fiction because he is reading contemporary authors for his own writing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bGY1zwr_wss?t=13215" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:15</a> | Can Psalm 82 be read as speaking to men because it says "you shall die like men"? | He says the comparison language does not force a human-only reading. | He warns that "like" comparisons do not prove identity. In Psalm 82, he sees the passage as amphibolous, blending divine-council language with human judges: the comparison to mortals can apply to divine beings who have abandoned their proper role and to human judges acting unjustly, so the phrase does not by itself settle the referent as only men. |
